topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom was thick with forced merriment and the cloying scent of expensive perfume. I watched from across the room, my jaw tight enough to crack stone. Then, there he was. Liam. He was laughing, a bright, unrestrained sound that scraped against my nerves like sandpaper. My stomach clenched, a cold, hard knot forming just beneath my ribs. I deliberately turned my attention to a particularly ornate floral arrangement, focusing intently on the wilting edges of a white rose, anything to avoid the inevitable collision. When he finally turned, our eyes met for a fraction of a second—a fleeting, unwelcome acknowledgment—and I immediately looked away, my gaze sharp and dismissive, as if he were a smudge on the polished floor.

[story 2]

I smoothed down my dress for the tenth time, a small, unnecessary ritual to maintain a façade of composure. Every step I took across the parquet floor felt weighted with resentment. I knew he was there, of course; mutual friends create unavoidable orbits. When I spotted him near the champagne fountain, a wave of intense irritation washed over me. I consciously slowed my pace, making sure my expression remained perfectly neutral, a blank canvas hiding the churning dissatisfaction inside. When we were forced into proximity by a shared acquaintance, my voice, when I offered a strained greeting, was deliberately clipped, devoid of any warmth. It was a performance, a tightrope walk over a chasm of old grievances.

[story 3]

The music swelled, a saccharine melody that grated on my already frayed nerves. I was leaning against a pillar, trying to appear absorbed in a conversation with a distant relative, but my focus kept snapping back to the entrance. There he stood, looking effortlessly charming, a stark contrast to the sour feeling that immediately flooded my system. A distinct tightening began in my chest, a physical reaction to the sight of him. I forced a slow, deliberate breath, trying to regulate the rapid pulse hammering beneath my skin. When our paths converged near the cake table, I offered a curt nod, my eyes flicking over him with an expression so cool and distant it probably looked like disdain. The silence that followed our brief exchange felt heavy, saturated with everything I wished to keep locked away.
